documentation.abstract: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because it is estimated to have an extremely small population size which is declining due to the deterioration of its habitat by introduced invasive plants, and to the presence of invasive non-native mammals impacting breeding success. Unfortunately it appears that the area of remaining habitat is still continuing to decline overall due to residential and associated infrastructure development. The species still requires intensive conservation efforts in order to persist.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

The species has long been legally protected in Mauritius. The Black River National Park covers a major part of the species' distribution. A species recovery programme was initiated by the Mauritian Wildlife Foundation in September 2005, in which territories identified in the most recent survey at the time were revisited and the breeding behaviour of these birds was closely monitored (Cole et al. 2006, R. Cole in litt. 2007). Between 2005 and 2008 an intensive management plan was applied, involving wild population monitoring, predator control at nest sites, rescue/harvest of wild nests, artificial incubation and hand-rearing of offspring, and a trial release of birds to the predator-free, restored offshore islet Ile aux Aigrettes in December 2006 (Cole et al. 2006, R. Cole in litt. 2007). All individuals from rescued nests were hand-reared by a team from Chester Zoo, the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust or the Mauritian Wildlife Foundation (Sørensen 2005).

A total of 38 individuals, all originating from wild nests, were released onto Ile aux Aigrettes between 2006 and 2009 (Cole et al. 2006, R. Cole in litt. 2007, R. G. Maggs in litt. 2010, Maggs et al. 2011, Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024). The Ile aux Aigrettes population is closely monitored and provided with supplementary food, which was reduced to once every other day in 2021, with no adverse effect on the population (G. Maggs in litt. 2010, Maggs et al. 2011, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024). The first fledglings were produced on Ile aux Aigrettes in 2008, and in 2010, 14 fledglings were produced from five breeding pairs, and the total population currently stands at 55 individuals (G. Maggs in litt. 2010, Maggs et al. 2011, Ferrière et al. 2015, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024). Releases and nest rescues have now ceased, and intensive research has been conducted to increase knowledge of the species and its current threats, and to investigate potential management techniques for long-term management of the species (G. Maggs in litt. 2016).

In 2021, six fledglings were released in the Ebony Forest Reserve by the Ebony Forest Reserve group after being collected and hand-reared, with the aim of creating a new subpopulation in the Chamarel region (Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw in litt. 2024). During its second season, hand-rearing was unsuccessful, and the program was paused in 2023 (Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024). If successful in the long-term, this will expand the species' distribution further to the southwest (S. Henshaw in litt. 2024).

In 2019, intense monitoring of the population by the Mauritian Wildlife Foundation in the Combo region began, focussing on obtaining data on numbers of territories and nesting success in the area, as past line transects for the species proved inefficient as the species' detectability is too low for such methods (Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024). Since then, monitoring has shifted once more to focus on population density, in order to improve detection of potential declines in the species' population in the area (Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024).

The creation of a c. 10ha predator-free "mainland island in Brise-Fer could be a potential translocation site for the species, and such an area could also be created within the species' existing range, and the possibility of this is indeed already been investigated, with rat control methods already having been trialled in the Combo area, with a similar 10 ha trap grid being established in the area in 2023 (Nichols et al. 2005, G. Maggs in litt. 2016, Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw in litt. 2024).

In addition, research into the demography and genetic health of the species' populations in Combo and on the Ile aux Aigrettes has started, to improve understanding of genetic diversity and carrying capacities in these areas (Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024).

In 2023, a workshop between the Mauritian Wildlife Foundation, the National Parks and Conservation Service and Ebony Forest Reserve was held, with the aim of producing a Mauritius Olive White-eye Conservation Plan, which was published at the end of 2023 (Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024).

Conservation Actions Proposed

Continue population monitoring and ringing in both the reintroduced population on Ile aux Aigrettes and in the wild mainland population to increase knowledge of the species' behaviour, habitat use and preferences and dispersal (G. Maggs in litt. 2016). Continue monitoring of accessible nests at Combo and on Ile aux Aigrettes (Maggs et al. 2011). Continue to search the Black River National Park and other suspected areas in Mauritius to identify any previously unknown sub-populations (Maggs et al. 2011). Continue rehabilitation of native forest in appropriate areas to improve food sources for the species, particularly by controlling non-native invasive plant species, and increasing the amount of native nectar-producing plant species (Raisin et al. 2023). Continue and increase the control of non-native invasive predators, such as rats, monkeys and crows (Raisin et al. 2023). Improve the security of smaller populations by raising awareness, identifying and protecting key sites for the species (inside and outside of the National Park), and mitigating threats in these sites (Raisin et al. 2023). Raise awareness of the impacts of residential, industrial and infrastructure development on the species and its habitat, within and outside of the National Park.
documentation.habitatAndEcology:
It is restricted to humid native upland forest habitats, and the extremely small population is now found only at elevations between 200-500 m. It feeds principally on nectar, but also insects and sometimes fruit, and travels considerable distances to productive flowers (Cheke 1987). Some introduced plant species have become extremely important nectar sources (Safford 1991). High densities may be associated with mosaics of small plantations of exotic trees, where nest predation may be low, interspersed with native vegetation for foraging (Safford 1997). The species has been found to be successful in forests dominated by non-native Syzygium jambos, whereas it does not adapt to forests dominated by other introduced species such as Psidium cattleianum or large areas of plantation (S. Henshaw in litt. 2024). The ongoing spread of these, in combination with the presence of invasive vertebrates, is causing an ongoing degradation of the species' habitat quality and likely extent.

The species defends territories of approximately 0.5 ha, and aggressively defends a favoured flower or nest-sites against conspecifics and Mauritius Grey White-eye Z. mauritianus (Nichols et al. 2005, Maggs et al. 2015). The clutch-size is 2-3 eggs (Safford 2013). In recent decades, pairs on the mainland have not generally fledged more than one offspring per nesting attempt and productivity is predicted at 0.2 fledglings per female per season. Productivity has been higher in the managed subpopulation on Ile aux Aigrettes, where pairs have been recorded fledging between 1 and 3 nestlings per nesting attempt (Maggs et al. 2015).
documentation.population:
New surveys have estimated the population size in the Combo area at 44 pairs in 2024 (20 in the tea fields and 24 in the forest [S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024]), which equates to 88 mature individuals. As it is present in a number of other areas of the National Park too, albeit at a lower density, the population has been estimated to be under 250 mature individuals in total (Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024), and therefore placed in the range of 100-250 mature individuals.

Following twenty years of conservation work on Ile aux Aigrettes, 16 white-eyes were released on the predator-free island between December 2006 and March 2007 (Cole et al. 2006, Anon 2006). A further release of c. 20 birds between 2007 and 2009 enabled the establishment of a small population on Ile aux Aigrettes, and birds have since successfully fledged young there every breeding season since 2008/09 (Maggs et al. 2011). In March 2015, the population on Ile aux Aigrettes stood at 46 individuals (Ferrière et al. 2015), and reached a peak of 60 individuals in 2016 (Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024). A current population of 55 individuals (roughly equivalent to 37 mature individuals) is estimated to be present on the Ile aux Aigrettes (Raisin et al. 2023), however as this reintroduced population is still dependent on conservation actions such as supplementary feeding this is considered to be a managed population and as such is not taken into account in the total population size at this time. There are concerns that numbers here may also be declining in part due to lack of genetic diversity (Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024).
documentation.populationTrend: The species has experienced declines in population size (Safford 1997, R. Switzer in litt. 2003, Nichols et al. 2004, Anon 2006, Ormsby et al. 2012, Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024) from 350 pairs (700 mature individuals) (Cheke 1987) in the mid-1970s, to approximately 100-250 mature individuals currently (Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024). The species is considered to be undergoing a continuing decline, due to the continued increase in proportion of non-native invasive plants within the species' habitat, and also to the impact of introduced predator and competitor species (S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024). As these threats are still ongoing and the conservation actions undertaken, although impactful, has not yet fully mitigated their impact, it is inferred that the species' overall population size is slowly decreasing. The rate of decline has not been quantified.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Zosterops chloronothos (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Zosterops chloronothus.
documentation.threats: The species suffered severe declines in the past due to deforestation for agriculture and development, with only 5% of Mauritius' native forests now remaining (van Balen and Sharpe 2020). The species has remained highly threatened due to habitat destruction and degradation as a result of the invasion of non-native plant species, with the density of its population decreasing where proportions of non-native plants are higher (Safford 2013, Raisin et al. 2023, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024). Predation from Black Rat Rattus rattus and Long-tailed Macaque Macaca fascicularis have been recorded (Safford 2013, Maggs et al. 2015), as has predation from other bird species (C. Jones in litt. 2000, Nichols et al. 2005): a study of the co-occurring Mauritius Grey White-eye Zosterops mauritianus found only 8% of nests resulted in fledglings, with predation by native Mauritius Bulbul Hypsipetes olivaceus and the invasive non-native Red-whiskered Bulbul Pycnonotus jocosus the most frequent cause of nest failure (Sørensen 2005). In addition, predation and disturbance from House Crows Corvus splendens has had an impact, in particular on the reintroduced population on Ile aux Aigrettes (Raisin et al. 2023, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024). The impact of rat predation on breeding productivity was previously predicted to immediately threaten the short-term viability of the species' mainland population, however this impact has been less severe than feared, as the inferred rate of decline has been slow in recent years (S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024). Future impacts include shifting rain patterns due to climate change, which may reduce food availability and, by consequent, breeding success (Raisin et al. 2023).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

geographicRange.narrative:
This species is endemic to Mauritius. Historically present to the north and east of its current range (Safford 2013), its small population is now confined to a small area in the southwest of the island, mainly restricted to the Black River Gorges National Park and surrounding areas (R. Switzer in litt. 2003, Nichols et al. 2004, Anon 2006, S. Henshaw in litt. 2024), including the Vallée des Couleurs, Alexander Falls, Pigeon Wood, Bel Ombre and Montagne Cocotte, with highest densities in the Combo area (C. Jones in litt. 2000, S. Henshaw, C. Jones and V. Tatayah in litt. 2024).

It is widespread in upland native forest, but largely absent from the whole Macchabé-Brise Fer area and Fouge Range (Safford 1997), with the species' core distribution having contracted since 1975, having disappeared from some outlying sites (Tamarin Falls, Jouanis and Monvert) and with the core area decreasing by at least 50% in 25 years (Nichols et al. 2005, Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2013). Its status in central Mauritius (Montagne Lagrave and the central plateau relics) remains uncertain (R. Switzer in litt. 2003, Nichols et al. 2004), although one was seen on Mt Lagrave in the breeding season in 2011/2012 (Ormsby et al. 2012, V. Tatayah in litt. 2012). Some individuals exploit isolated habitats over a wide area of the central plateau, including many relict patches of native vegetation (Safford 1997).

The species was reintroduced on Ile aux Aigrettes between December 2006 and March 2007 (Cole et al. 2006, Anon 2006), with a further release of approximately 20 birds between 2007 and 2010. The small population on Ile aux Aigrettes have successfully bred every breeding season since 2008/09 (Maggs et al. 2011), although the population size has experienced a small decline since its peak in 2016 (Mauritian Wildlife Foundation 2024).

documentation.habitatAndEcology: Ashy-bellied White-eye occupies a variety of habitats. These include scrub, forest edge, secondary growth, primary and secondary forest, heavily degraded forest, open woodland, monsoon thickets, lightly wooded cultivation, scrubby farmland, mangroves and coastal casuarinas (Casuarina). The species is found on medium to very small islands, from sea-level up to 1000 m on Alor and 1200 m on Timor. Subspecies citrinella has been reported up to 2000 m (van Balen 2019).
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be generally common in the Lesser Sundas, although scarce in the Torres Strait Islands (Flegg and Madge 1995, Coates and Bishop 1997).
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat destruction and fragmentation, and possibly from trapping for the cage-bird trade.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Zosterops palbebrosus unicus has been transferred to Ashy-bellied White-eye Z. citrinella, following genetic analysis by Lim et al. (2019).
documentation.threats: Ashy-bellied White-eye is threatened by deforestation and trapping for the cage-bird trade, although the extent of these threats is currently unknown.
documentation.useAndTrade: The Ashy-bellied White-eye may be trapped and traded for the cage-bird trade like related species, Indian White-eye and Mountain White-eye. A TRAFFIC report recognized the large trade in many Zosterops species in South-east Asia, however evidence is lacking for this specific species (Eaton et al. 2017).

geographicRange.narrative: There are four subspecies within Zosterops citrinella (Ashy-bellied White-eye). These are: citrinella, harterti, albiventris and unicus. They occur on the islands of the Lesser Sundas of Indonesia and Timor-Leste, and some small islets of Australia. Subspecies citrinella occurs on Sumba, Savu, Timor, Semau and Roti; subspecies harterti occurs on Lembata and Alor; and subspecies albiventris occurs on Lucipara, Gunungapi, Wetar, Romang, Damar, Teun, Kisar, Leti, Moa, Luang, Sermata, Babar, Tanimbar islands and islets in the Torres Strait off the extreme north-eastern coast of Australia (van Balen 2019a). Subspecies unicus occurs on Sumbawa and Flores (van Balen 2019b).

documentation.habitatAndEcology:
There are 15 subspecies within the Mountain White-eye (Zosterops japonicus). These are: whiteheadi, halconensis, parkesi, pectoralis, diutiae, volcani, difficilis, montanus, obstinatus, japonicus, insularis, loochooensis, daitoensis, stejnegeri and alani.

The subspecies whiteheadi, halconensis, parkesi, pectoralis, diutiae, volcani, difficilis, montanus and obstinatus usually occupy primary montane or moss forest, as well as ericaceous shrubs, forest edge, secondary growth, and in isolated bushes almost up to barren mountain summits, or as high as the fumes, fires and eruptions of volcanoes permit vegetation to approach. On Timor, Java and Luzon islands they also occupy casuarina (Casuarina) stands and pine (Pinus) forests. On Sumbawa island, they can be found in dodonaea shrubs, and on Flores island, in wooded cultivation. These subspecies are generally found no lower than 500 m, generally above 900–1,200 m; above 1,000 m in Philippines, 2,200–3,100 m in Sumatra, 1,600–3,300 m in Java, 1,500–3,500 m (occasionally down to 900 m) on Sulawesi; fairly common between 1,000 m and 1,300 m, common above 1,000 m to at least 2,300 m on Sumbawa, scarce at 2,300 m on Seram. The lowest mountain on Java on which it is known to occur is Mt Papandayan (2,660 m) (van Balen 2019a).

The subspecies japonicus, insularis, loochooensis, daitoensis, stejnegeri and alani occupy deciduous or mixed forests, thickets, open woodland, secondary growth, and also cultivated areas (e.g. urban parks, gardens, farmlands, groves, orchards). They can be found in tall forest trees in winter. They mostly breed in lowlands, but occur up to c. 1,000 m (van Balen 2019b).
documentation.population: No population estimate has been made, however the species is the result of merging two species that were common in much of their range and so it is likely to have a very large global population.
documentation.populationTrend: The population trend of this species is unknown, however it is not thought to be decreasing sufficiently rapidly for the species to be threatened.
documentation.taxonomicNotes:
Zosterops japonicus has been split into Z. japonicus and Z. simplex, and Z. montanus has been lumped with the resulting Z. japonicus following detailed work on genetics, morphology and vocalisations (Round et al. 2017, Lim et al. 2019).

Subspecies Z. j. hainanus has been transferred to Z. simplex (Handbook of the Birds of the World 2019, Lim et al. 2019).
documentation.threats: This species may be being trapped for the cage-bird trade.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species may be being trapped for the pet trade.

geographicRange.narrative: This species has a large range. The subspecies whiteheadi, halconensis, parkesi, pectoralis, diutiae and volcani are present in the Phillipines (van Balen 2019a). Subspecies obstinatus, dificilis and montanus are present in Indonesia; montanus is also present in Timor-Leste (van Balen 2019b). Subspecies japonicus, insularis, loochooensis, daitoensis, stejnegeri and alani are present in Japan; japonicus is also present in the southern part of South Korea (van Balen 2019a).

documentation.habitatAndEcology: Indian White-eye occupies deciduous, broadleaf evergreen/semi-evergreen and swamp-forests. It can also be found in forest edge, secondary growth, woodland, farmland, thickets, scrub and wooded cultivation (orchards, parks, gardens, cardamon plantations), open strand woodland, coastal scrub of all kinds, as well as mature and regenerating mangroves. Along the Gulf of Thailand coast, it is confined to belt of mangroves and scrub. This species is apparently the only passeriform found in the mangroves of the Sind coast in Pakistan. The species occurs from coastal areas up to 2,500 m, and in the Himalayas, up to 4,000 m; however it is most commonly found between 200 and 1,600 m (van Balen 2019).
documentation.population: There is no population size data available for this species, however based on the range size the population is thought to be very large.
documentation.populationTrend: Despite a lack of evidence, the species is precautionarily assessed as decreasing due to the trapping and trade of the species for the cage-bird market.
documentation.taxonomicNotes:
Oriental White-eye Zosterops palpebrosus has been split into Indian White-eye Z. palpebrosus, Hume's White-eye Z. auriventer and Sangkar White-eye Z. melanurus on the basis of thorough morphological comparisons (Wells et al. 2017a, b) and genetic differentiation, morphology and vocalisations (Round et al. 2017, Lim et al. 2019).

Z. p. unicus has been transferred to Ashy-bellied White-eye Z. citrinella, and Z. p. wiliamsoni has been transferred to Swinhoe's White-eye Z. simplex (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2019) following genetic analysis by Lim et al. (2019).
documentation.threats: The species is threatened by trapping for the cage-bird trade.
documentation.useAndTrade: Frequently trapped and traded within and to some degree beyond its range. No knowledge of numbers trapped from the wild or sustainability of trapping.

documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is described as common (Fry and Keith 2000).
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.

documentation.habitatAndEcology:
There are five subspecies within Swinhoe's White-eye (Zosterops simplex). These are: simplex, hainanus, erwini, williamsoni and salvadorii.

Z. s simplex and Z. s. hainanus occupy deciduous or mixed forests, thickets, open woodland, secondary growth, as well as cultivated areas (e.g. urban parks, gardens, farmlands, groves, orchards). In winter, they may occupy tall forest trees. They breed mainly in lowlands. They occur up to 800 m in southern China and up to 2,590 m in south-east Asia in non-breeding areas (van Balen 2019a). Z. s erwini and Z. s. williamsoni occupy deciduous, broadleaf evergreen/semi-evergreen and swamp-forest, as well as forest edge, secondary growth, woodland, farmland, thickets, all zones of mangroves (mature and regenerating), scrub and wooded cultivation (orchards, parks, gardens, cardamon plantations). They can also be found in open strand woodland, especially with stands of casuarinas, and coastal scrub of all kinds. They generally occur between 200–1600 m, but also locally in true lowlands. In Borneo, Z. s. erwinii is restricted to mangroves, swamp-forest and non-forest (edges of wet rice fields and scrub along riverbanks), mainly adjoining coastal areas. Along Gulf of Thailand coast, Z. s. williamsoni is confined to belt of mangroves and scrub (van Balen 2019b). Z. s. salvadorii inhabits coconut groves and other lowland wooded areas on Enggano Island (van Balen 2019c).
documentation.population: Exact population size estimates for this species are not available, however it is described as common in most of the range. Indeed, it is the most common bird in secondary forest on Hong Kong and also the most popular cage-bird in China (van Balen 2019a). It is abundant on Taiwan. The subspecies Z. s. williamsoni is considered common around the Gulf of Thailand and Z. s. erwini is common in peninsular Malaysia, but scarce in Malaysian Borneo and Brunei. On Sumatra the species was common, but there are concerns that the very high numbers of individuals trapped may be impacting the population. Subspecies Z. s. salvadorii is abundant on Enggano (Eaton et al. 2016).
documentation.populationTrend: An overall slow decline may be inferred due to the high levels of trapping.
documentation.taxonomicNotes:
Zosterops japonicus has been split into Z. japonicus and Z. simplex, and Z. salvadorii has been lumped into Z. simplex following detailed work on genetics, morphology and vocalisations (Round et al. 2017, Lim et al. 2019).

Subspecies Z. j. hainanus, Z. palpebrosus williamsoni and Z. p. erwini (previously included within Z. p. auriventer) have been transferred to Z. simplex (Handbook of the Birds of the World 2019).
documentation.threats: Despite very high levels of trapping in some parts of the species’s range, it remains abundant in many places.
documentation.useAndTrade: The species is a very popular cage-bird in China (van Balen 2019, a.).

geographicRange.narrative: Swinhoe's White-eye is abundant on Taiwan. The subspecies Z. s. williamsoni is considered common around the Gulf of Thailand and Z. s. erwini is common in peninsular Malaysia, but scarce in Malaysian Borneo and Brunei. On Sumatra, Indonesia, the species was common, but there are concerns that the very high numbers of individuals trapped may be impacting the population. Subspecies Z. s. salvadorii is abundant in wooded areas on Enggano Island, Indonesia (Eaton et al. 2016). The subspecies Z. s. hainanus is present on Hainan island, China (van Balen 2019a.).

documentation.abstract: Although this species has a small range, and possibly a moderately small population size, there are no indications that it is undergoing any decline. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

There are some community-based conservation projects on Ranongga.

Conservation Actions Proposed

Continue to monitor rates of forest loss using remote sensing data. Calculate relative population densities in various habitats. Support sustainable forest-use programmes. Publicise this species as endemic to Ranongga.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: It appears to be common in forest and forest edge, but also in old-growth forest, regrowth and thickets with scattered old trees (Gibbs 1996, G. Dutson pers. obs. 1998, Dutson 2011, van Balen 2020, eBird 2021). Observations in 1998 suggested it may not survive in cleared and scrubby habitats far away from forest or without remnant large trees (G. Dutson pers. obs. 1998). It sometimes feeds in flowers of coconuts (Dutson 2011).
documentation.population: The population size is unknown. It was previously suspected to number 2,500-9,999 mature individuals, but this value is unsubstantiated. The species has been described as fairly common (Dutson 2011) and most eBird checklists (see eBird 2021) indicate that it is common throughout the island, including in villages.
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be fairly stable. Although there has been a slow reduction in forest cover—equivalent to c.5% over the past 10 years, dropping to a rate of c.3%/10 years between 2016 and 2020 (Global Forest Watch [2022], using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein)—this species, like most other Zosterops, is tolerant of habitat degradation and occurs in second growth and gardens (Dutson 2011, van Balen 2020, eBird 2021). Consequently, an axiomatic population reduction in response to small-scale habitat degradation cannot be assumed and the population is suspected to be stable.
documentation.threats: Forest is largely restricted to steep slopes and rocky, hilly areas on Ranongga. Gardens are used to grow food for the nearby urban market on Ghizo as well as for Ranongga villages, and the expansion of this land use was a previous cause of suspected decline. However, following a tsunami in 2007, the island has been raised by c.2 m, providing suitable areas for gardening that were previously underwater (C. Filardi in litt. 2012). Much of the remaining forest is on terrain unsuitable for gardening, although trees are likely to be felled for local timber demand. Forest cover loss has continued at a slow rate (equivalent to c.5% over the past 10 years; Global Forest Watch [2021], using data and methods from Hansen et al. [2013]), however it is unclear whether this has driven population declines given this species's tolerance of degradation. Given the species' small range, climate change may be a future threat if it proves susceptible to increasingly frequent storms.

geographicRange.narrative: Zosterops splendidus is endemic to Ranongga (= Ganongga) in the Solomon Islands.

documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified.
documentation.populationTrend: This species is considered to have a medium dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 23.4 % within its mapped range over the past 10 years (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). Therefore, as a precautionary measure, it is tentatively suspected that this loss of cover may have led to a decline of between 1-19 % in the species' population size over the same time frame.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Zosterops pallidus and Z. virens (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Z. pallidus following Dowsett and Forbes-Watson (1993) and Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species has been reported as used in trade at a low prevalence, being found in two of the seven trade datasets evaluated (Donald et al. 2024).
